serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Imam Bayildi is approximately 350 g, comprising one or two halved eggplants stuffed with onion, capsicum, tomato, and cheese, served with bread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11210) and component ingredients (onion, tomato, capsicum, olive oil, cheese) from USDA SR Legacy; composite estimation for Imam Bayildi
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Imam Bayildi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked eggplant, sautéed onion, cooked tomato, cooked capsicum, olive oil, and part-skim mozzarella cheese, reflecting the dish composition described in the input record. The Singapore restaurant version includes a thin layer of cheese, which is incorporated into the estimate. Bread served alongside is excluded from per-100g values as it is a side item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for eggplant / aubergine dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Imam Bayildi. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked eggplant (GI ~15, low category) and the low-GI profile of the vegetable-dominant stuffing (onion, tomato, capsicum). The thin cheese layer and olive oil further suppress glycemic response. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (15 × 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 3.5) / 100 = 4.8 g. This is a low GL. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cheese and added salt in preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the cheese layer and seasoning in Imam Bayildi contribute approximately 185 mg sodium per 100 g; a full restaurant serving (~350 g) may deliver ~650 mg sodium, representing about 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and portion control or requesting reduced-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high vegetable and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Imam Bayildi has a low estimated glycemic index (~15) and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.8), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) from eggplant and vegetables further moderates postprandial glucose response, though the accompanying bread should be accounted for in carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominant fat in Imam Bayildi is monounsaturated fat from olive oil (~3.2 g/100 g), which is associated with reduced LDL cholesterol; saturated fat is low (~1.4 g/100 g) from the thin cheese layer, and the dish's high vegetable content provides antioxidants and fibre beneficial for cardiovascular health, making it a generally heart-friendly option when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Imam Bayildi (İmam Bayıldı in Turkish, literally 'the imam fainted') is a classic Ottoman and Turkish dish consisting of whole eggplants (aubergines) that are halved or slit lengthwise, lightly fried or roasted, and then stuffed generously with a fragrant mixture of sautéed onions, garlic, capsicum (bell pepper), and tomatoes cooked in olive oil. The name is said to derive from the legend that an imam fainted with delight — or shock at the quantity of olive oil used — upon tasting the dish. In the Singapore restaurant version served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant, the dish is baked with a thin layer of melted cheese on top and served with bread, giving it a slightly richer profile than the traditional Turkish preparation. The eggplant flesh becomes silky and tender during baking, absorbing the sweet, herbed tomato-onion filling. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet from caramelised onion and tomato, with a subtle richness from olive oil and cheese. Nutritionally, Imam Bayildi is a vegetable-dominant dish with moderate energy (~98 kcal/100 g), low saturated fat, and a meaningful fibre contribution from eggplant and vegetables. The olive oil base provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~15) and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. Sodium content is moderate due to cheese and seasoning. It is classified as vegetarian and halal-friendly. The dish is a staple of Middle Eastern and Turkish cuisine and is widely recognised across the Levant, Greece, and the broader Mediterranean region.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Imam Bayildi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy component ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked eggplant. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-documented, the component ingredients are individually well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients (cooked eggplant, sautéed onion, cooked tomato, cooked capsicum, olive oil, part-skim mozzarella). No single approved-source entry for Imam Bayildi was found. 2. The Singapore restaurant version includes a thin cheese layer not present in traditional Turkish recipes; this is reflected in the saturated fat, calcium, and sodium estimates. 3. Bread served alongside is excluded from per-100g values as it is a side accompaniment. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component ingredient sources used. 5. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the ingredient profile (olive oil, vegetables, natural cheese). 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked eggplant; no direct GI measurement for Imam Bayildi was found in the approved GI literature. 8. The cuisine tag 'Middle Eastern' is retained from the input; the dish is specifically of Turkish/Ottoman origin but is widely adopted across the Middle East and Mediterranean. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific dish in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
